content:
I’m an MSc Operational Research with Data Science graduate from the University of Edinburgh, UK. Currently, I work at Sopra Steria as a Data Scientist to solve data science and engineering problems dealing with clients such as NatWest Group. I am thoroughly skilled in Python, SQL, Julia, and R.
I have listed some projects I worked on during my MSc and my work. I deeply understand Machine Learning (Logistic Regression, Decision Trees, Bagging/Boosting, Clustering, PCA, Mixed Integer Programming, Linear Programming, Greedy Search, and Local Search heuristics). I am highly proficient in SQL, Python (NumPy, pandas, scikit-learn, TensorFlow, PySpark), R (shiny, ggplot2, dplyr, randomForest), Anaconda (virtual environments, Jupyter Lab, Jupyter notebook), and Optimisation (Pyomo, FICO Xpress, Gurobipy, Pulp). I am also experienced in big data using Hadoop, Hive and Spark, bash scripts, git for version control, and docker containers for deployment. Finally, I am familiar with Atlassian products such as JIRA, Confluence, and Bitbucket.
I am looking for opportunities to apply my Machine learning and Optimisation skills to solve challenging problems. Please feel free to reach me through my email: ijolly1997[at]gmail[dot]com or LinkedIn!
Interests
Operational Research Optimisation Machine Learning Data Analytics Reinforcement Learning Causal Inference Bayesian Statistics Education
MSc in Operational Research with Data Science, 2020-21 University of Edinburgh
Further Statistics for Economics and Econometrics, 2019 London School of Economics BSc (Hons) in Mathematics, 2015-19 University of Delhi
summary: I’m an MSc Operational Research with Data Science graduate from the University of Edinburgh, UK. Currently, I work at Sopra Steria as a Data Scientist to solve data science and engineering problems dealing with clients such as NatWest Group.
title: Ishaan Jolly

content:
2022 has been an eventful year in my professional life but a miserable one in my personal life. I have had a chance to reflect on the past many times throughout the year, and keeping my emotions at bay has been a challenge; nevertheless, I have managed to heal a few wounds, but some are still at large. One of the essential things I learned was through my manager at work, “imagine you have a bucket, and everything you feel is poured into it like drops of water”. There will be a time when the bucket will overflow, so I want to start with the times when it burst and how I bought it under control through the positive news afterwards. I will also discuss some resolutions that I want to exercise this year.
Losing people: No, I have not lost these people from the material world but from life. Four people meant a lot to me, and I lost all of them in a week. I miss them a lot and often think about the good times I had through photos, places I visited and even conversations I have with someone else. I feel apologetic, angry, and regretful because these people put meaning into the city where I am now based. I feel empty and in a mirage because of it. How did I cope?: It’s been challenging to cope with this and with this massive emptiness. I can only move forward by forgetting about it and keeping myself busy.
What more can I do to repair? : Sadly, I cannot do anything anymore. The best I can do is to wish well for them and, at the same time, keep hope alive (i.e. by keeping my fingers crossed).
Anxiety: I have avoided reading emails or opening my social media. I trace this back to an email I received a year ago. I leave opening them for days, or if I have to open my account, I restrict myself to only sending an email and not reading any emails. How did I cope?: When urgent, I usually turn to some folks who I trust and ask them to be my support when I read through my emails. I worry about this turning into a habit.
What more can I do to repair? I need to be more resilient to both good and bad news. I recall my mentor at my work mentioning, “you need to remain immune to negative and positive communication”. It does not take time for a negative aspect to become positive and vice-versa. I thought it meant for me to be a robot, so I did not pay heed. However, on reflection, I now realise what he meant, and perhaps it is a life skill I need to develop to get this resilience.
Family: I have realised that I have avoided my family on occasions when my work takes over me. I avoid calls and do not respond to messages. How did I cope?: I did not cope well, and in fact, I realised that the more I avoid my family, the more I am heading towards loneliness. I don’t have much to look forward to besides my family, and I must take time out for them. Active Listening: For my end-year review this year, one of my colleagues point out that I should actively work towards active listening. He noted a couple of instances when I would ask to repeat or forget, requesting some information I needed for the task. I found some helpful suggestions after going through some articles: listen, don’t interrupt, refer to non-verbal cues, stay focused during and paraphrase and summarise. I plan to use these aspects for self-improvement.
Professionalism: In my first year in a corporate, I have had personal troubles navigating through what is professional and what is not. I would often go into the office, seeing varied behaviours. In my one-to-one with my manager, he mentioned that time is essential in determining the kind of relationship people want for you and the type of relationship I would like to have with them. Often, I would spend time giving my time to people only to be in their good books, but I now realise that I do not only want to be a helpful or a giving person. I want to build credibility for myself as it would do me more good in the long run. So, to act upon this, I have become sterner and less social (not aloof) to the point that it works for me and does not impact my work.
Getting out: This year, I have been out considerably more than I have in the past. I like coding and learning, which means often spending time on screen. My landlady pointed out that it is detrimental to one’s health. So, I picked up cycling, which I enjoyed a lot. I plan to continue this hobby and supplement it further by taking another outdoor sport such as running, badminton, or swimming. I took this photo while cycling from Marchmont to Peebles, with a stopover at Penicuik for Pizza!
Time towards what I like: I like optimisation and developing software devoted to it. Since being part of two giant corporates, my schedule has led to skipping my interests. This would be a resolution to establish this year as the rest of the points. I hope to produce open-source code and participate in things of interest. Further, this year delivering two sessions I enjoy teaching, so I will make it a point to provide courses on optimisation and programming.
summary: 2022 has been an eventful year in my professional life but a miserable one in my personal life. I have had a chance to reflect on the past many times throughout the year, and keeping my emotions at bay has been a challenge; nevertheless, I have managed to heal a few wounds, but some are still at large.
title: Reflections & Resolutions: 2022

summary: The talk explored various classes of Machine Learning namely Supervised, Unsupervised and Reinforcement Learning, accompanying each class with an example. The talk ended with my case study on my client project involving forecasting (regression) and classification model for propensity prediction.
title: Machine Learning - A review and case study

summary: The talk aimed to introduce various clustering methods for different data types, in addition to exploring ways to understand and finding ways to improve the perfomance of clustering algorithms whilst perusal of best metrics.
title: Clustering - Introduction

content:
Create slides in Markdown with Wowchemy Wowchemy | Documentation
Features Efficiently write slides in Markdown 3-in-1: Create, Present, and Publish your slides Supports speaker notes Mobile friendly slides Controls Next: Right Arrow or Space Previous: Left Arrow Start: Home Finish: End Overview: Esc Speaker notes: S Fullscreen: F Zoom: Alt + Click PDF Export Code Highlighting Inline code: variable
Code block:
porridge = "blueberry" if porridge == "blueberry": print("Eating...") Math In-line math: $x + y = z$
Block math:
$$ f\left( x \right) = ;\frac{{2\left( {x + 4} \right)\left( {x - 4} \right)}}{{\left( {x + 4} \right)\left( {x + 1} \right)}} $$
Fragments Make content appear incrementally
{{% fragment %}} One {{% /fragment %}} {{% fragment %}} **Two** {{% /fragment %}} {{% fragment %}} Three {{% /fragment %}} Press Space to play!
One Two Three A fragment can accept two optional parameters:
class: use a custom style (requires definition in custom CSS) weight: sets the order in which a fragment appears Speaker Notes Add speaker notes to your presentation
{{% speaker_note %}} - Only the speaker can read these notes - Press `S` key to view {{% /speaker_note %}} Press the S key to view the speaker notes!
Only the speaker can read these notes Press S key to view Themes black: Black background, white text, blue links (default) white: White background, black text, blue links league: Gray background, white text, blue links beige: Beige background, dark text, brown links sky: Blue background, thin dark text, blue links night: Black background, thick white text, orange links serif: Cappuccino background, gray text, brown links simple: White background, black text, blue links solarized: Cream-colored background, dark green text, blue links Custom Slide Customize the slide style and background
{{< slide background-image="/media/boards.jpg" >}} {{< slide background-color="#0000FF" >}} {{< slide class="my-style" >}} Custom CSS Example Let’s make headers navy colored.
Create assets/css/reveal_custom.css with:
.reveal section h1, .reveal section h2, .reveal section h3 { color: navy; } Questions? Ask
Documentation
summary: An introduction to using Wowchemy's Slides feature.

summary: Designed model for rebalancing and scheduling of Just Eat bikes in Edinburgh through a combination of FICO Xpress and Python for implementation and R for historic analysis.
title: Bike Sharing

summary: Designed a simulation for a typical laptop manufacturing process using Simul8 and Excel for input and output analysis. Using testing and analysis, suggested improvements to increase the throughput and  better the manufacturing process
title: Laptop Manufacturing Process

summary: This project involved suggesting a time-dependent facility location and scheduling model such that every person within Scotland visits the nearest vaccination centre, according to a certain priority group and that the vaccinations are received according to age wise index and within a certain period as laid out by health board authorities (NHS Scotland).
title: Optimal allocation of population to vaccination centres

summary: Conducted a mini case study on a paediatric vaccine pricing model for Centres of Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), using a combination of technologies such as NEOS server, BARON and Python to optimise the pricing of DTaP vaccines in private and public sectors in the US. Through a series of testing and running simulations, various strategies were developed for CDC in case of vaccine deficiency and market segmentation.
title: Paediatric Vaccine Pricing

summary: Analysed various biomarkers using health data to understand and predict the causes behind diabetes and eGFR over a period in R. Finally, fitted a regression model with the best set of predictors and performed 10 folds cross-validation to validate the AUC.
title: Predicting Cause of Diabetes

summary: Analysed the reasons behind cancellation of hotel bookings through a series of exploratory data analysis, which was followed by feature engineering and various classification models. Achieved an accuracy of 0.82 through a random forest model implementation.
title: Predicting Hotel Cancellation
